blocks.text: Psalm 149
Praise the Lord! Praise the Lord from the heavens; praise him in the heights!

Ps 148: Let heaven and earth praise the Lord. The third of the five hymns that make up the doxology concluding the Psalms. More formal than the two preceding psalms, it employs the verb “to praise” twelve times, “ ” four times, and “the name (of the )” three times; it is neatly composed of two almost equal panels, vv. 1–6, introduced by “Praise the from the heavens,” and vv. 7–14, introduced by “Praise the from the earth.” Unlike other hymns, it focuses on the groups invited to praise, the inhabitants of heaven (vv. 1–4), and the inhabitants of earth (vv. 7–12). The divine acts meriting praise are mentioned only briefly: for the first group (vv. 5–6), the motive for giving praise is that the created them and assigned them all their tasks; for the second (v. 13), the motive is that the ’s name and glory are exalted above heaven and earth.
Praise him, all his angels; praise him, all his host!
Praise him, sun and moon; praise him, all you shining stars!
3 : This may recall other ancient cultures, in which astral bodies were deities.
Praise him, you highest heavens, and you waters above the heavens!
Let them praise the name of the Lord, for he commanded and they were created.
5 : , the psalm recognizes here and elsewhere the orderly Priestly story of creation by word in Gen 1, emphasizing that God continues to have this role in the universe.
He established them forever and ever; he fixed their bounds, which cannot be passed.
Praise the Lord from the earth, you sea monsters and all deeps,
fire and hail, snow and frost, stormy wind fulfilling his command!
7–8 : Somewhat autonomous deities in surrounding cultures, but under God’s strict control here.
Mountains and all hills, fruit trees and all cedars!
Wild animals and all cattle, creeping things and flying birds!
Kings of the earth and all peoples, princes and all rulers of the earth!
Young men and women alike, old and young together!
Let them praise the name of the Lord, for his name alone is exalted; his glory is above earth and heaven.
He has raised up a horn for his people, praise for all his faithful, for the people of Israel who are close to him. Praise the Lord!
14 : , a symbol of strength. After all nature and all the peoples of the earth are summoned to give praise to the , Israel is mentioned in climactic place as his special people.
